a1_01.text: am / is / are - forms of the verb to be
a1_01.note: I am, he is, they are
a1_01.exp:
to be is the most important verb in the English language, an analogue of the Russian “to be/appear”.

Three forms: am - only with I; is - with he, she, it; are - with you, we, they.

⚠️ An important difference from Russian: in English the verb cannot be missed. In Russian we say “I am a student,” and in English we must say: I am a student.
a1_01.exc: Short forms: I'm, you're, he's, she's, it's, we're, they're. In short answers the abbreviation is prohibited: Yes, I am. (not Yes, I'm)
mistakes: She be a teacher → She is a teacher
I am hungry → I am hungry
Skip to be: I student → I am a student
ex: I am hungry.
I'm hungry.
She is a doctor.
She's a doctor.
They are ready.
They're ready.
It is cold today.
It's cold today.

a1_02.note: I'm not tired. He isn't here. They aren't ready.
a1_02.exp:
Negation is constructed by adding not: am not, is not, are not.
Short forms: isn't (= is not), aren't (= are not). Only am not is not reduced to amn't - an exception!
mistakes: I amn't tired → I'm not tired (amn't does not exist!)
He don't tired → He isn't tired
She is not = shes not - only she's not or she isn't
ex: I'm not a student.
I'm not a student.
He isn't tired.
He's not tired.
We aren't ready.
We're not ready.

a1_03.text: Questions with to be: Am I? / Is she? / Are they?
a1_03.note: Is she a teacher? Are you cold? Am I late?
a1_03.exp:
In questions, the verb to be is placed before the subject: She is → Is she?

Special questions: question word + to be + subject: Where is he? What is this?

Short answers: Yes, I am. / No, she isn't
mistakes: Is you cold? → Are you cold?
She is late? - question without inversion: Is she late?
Where he is? → Where is he?
ex: Are you a student?
Are you a student?
Is it expensive?
Is it expensive?
Where are they?
Where are they?

a1_04.text: Short answers: Yes, I am. / No, she isn't.
a1_04.note: Only full form - not allowed: "Yes, I'm"
a1_04.exp:
In short answers the pronoun + to be. You cannot shorten the form of the verb in an affirmative answer.
Are you tired? — Yes, I am.✓    Yes, I'm. ✗
mistakes: Yes, I'm → Yes, I am (cannot be abbreviated in an affirmative answer)
No, she is'nt → No, she isn't
ex: Is she ready? — Yes, she is.
Is she ready? - Yes.
Are they your friends? — No, they aren't.
Are they your friends? - No.

a1_05.note: a dog, an apple, an hour, a university
a1_05.exp:
a and an are the indefinite article, placed before the first mentioned object in the singular.

The choice depends on the sound, not the letter:
• a - before the consonant sound: a book, a car, a university [ju...]
• an - before a vowel sound: an apple, an hour [aʊ...], an honest man
a1_05.exc: a university, a European - although they begin with a vowel, the initial sound is [j], therefore a. an hour, an honest - h is mute, the initial sound is a vowel.
mistakes: an university → a university (sound [j])
a apple → an apple
a honest man → an honest man (h mute)
ex: I saw a cat in the garden.
I saw a cat in the garden.
She is an engineer.
She's an engineer.
It took an hour.
It took an hour.

a1_06.note: the sun, the door, the book on the table
a1_06.exp:
the is used when the speaker and listener know what they are talking about.

When we put the:
1. The subject has already been mentioned:I saw a cat. The cat was black.
2. One of a kind: the sun, the moon, the earth
3. It is clear from the context: Close the window, please.
4. Superlative: the best, the biggest
a1_06.exc: Pronunciation: the [ðə] before consonants, [ði] before vowels. In oral speech, often [ðɪ] for emphasis.
mistakes: <s>I like the music</s> → I like music (general meaning - no article)
<s>The life is short</s> → Life is short
He is the doctor → He is a doctor (First mentioned)
ex: The film we saw was amazing.
The movie we watched was amazing.
Could you pass the salt?
Could you pass the salt?

a1_07.text: Zero article - when the article is not needed
a1_07.note: I like music. She plays tennis. He is from Russia.
a1_07.exp:
The article is not placed before:
• Proper names: John, London, Russia
• Languages and nationalities: English, Russian
• Sports and games: football, chess
• Food/drinks in a general sense: I love coffee
• Abstract concepts in general: Life is short. Love is blind.
• Plural in the general sense: Dogs are friendly.
mistakes: <s>I play the tennis</s> → I play tennis
<s>She speaks the Spanish</s> → She speaks Spanish
<s>the Russia</s> → Russia (countries without an article, except The USA, The UK)
ex: She speaks Spanish.
She speaks Spanish.
He plays basketball every day.
He plays basketball every day.

a1_08.note: I work. He works. She goes. It runs.
a1_08.exp:
Present Simple is used for: habits and regular actions, facts and general truths, schedules.

Formula: I/you/we/they + infinitive; he/she/it + infinitive+s/es

Rules for writing endings -s/-es:
• Most verbs: + s → works, plays
• Ending in -o, -ch, -sh, -s, -ss, -x: + es → goes, watches, washes
• End with a consonant + -y: -y → ies → studies, tries
a1_08.exc: Exceptions: have → has, be → is, do → does, go → goes
a1_08.tip: Time markers: always, usually, often, sometimes, rarely, never, every day/week.
mistakes: She work → She works (forgot -s for 3rd person)
He go → He goes (go → goes)
She studies → She studies (consonant + y → ies)

markers.note: Frequency markers come before the main verb, but after to be.

ex: I drink coffee every morning.
I drink coffee every morning.
She works in a hospital.
She works in a hospital.
The Earth goes round the Sun.
The Earth revolves around the Sun.

a1_09.note: I don't like it. He doesn't like it. (not: he don't)
a1_09.exp:
Negation is constructed using the auxiliary verb do/does + not:
• I/you/we/they + don't + infinitive
• he/she/it + doesn't + infinitive

⚠️ After doesn't the main verb without -s! She doesn't like (not likes).
mistakes: She doesn't likes → She doesn't like (after doesn't - the base form!)
He don't work → He doesn't work
I don't like → I don't like

markers.note: The same markers as for affirmative sentences.

ex: I don't eat meat.
I don't eat meat.
He doesn't speak French.
He doesn't speak French.
They don't work here.
They don't work here.

a1_10.text: Present Simple - questions: Do you? /Does she?
a1_10.note: Do you like pizza? Does he work here? Where do they live?
a1_10.exp:
Questions: Do/Does + subject + infinitive?
• I/you/we/they: Do you...?
• he/she/it: Does she...?

Special questions: question word + do/does + subject + infinitive:
Where does she live? What do they do?

⚠️ Exception: the question “Who?” without do: Who likes ice cream?
mistakes: Does she like? → Does she like?
Do he work? → Does he work?
<s>Where does she live?</s> → Where does she live?

markers.note: In questions, markers come after the subject.

ex: Do you speak Russian?
Do you speak Russian?
Does she live nearby?
Does she live nearby?
Where do you work?
Where do you work?

a1_11.text: Stative verbs - verbs not used in Continuous
a1_11.note: I know, not: I am knowing. I love, not: I am loving.
a1_11.exp:
Some verbs describe a state rather than an action - they are not used in Continuous tenses.

Main groups:
• Opinions: know, believe, think, understand, remember, forget
• Feelings: love, hate, like, want, need, prefer
• Perception: see, hear, smell, taste, look
• Being: be, exist, belong, contain, seem, appear
a1_11.exc: think/have/see/taste can be used in the Continuous, but with a different meaning: I think (opinion) vs I'm thinking (thinking right now).
mistakes: I am knowing → I know
She is loving him → She loves him
He is wanting a coffee → He wants a coffee
ex: I understand you. (не: I am understanding)
I understand you.
She loves chocolate. (не: is loving)
She loves chocolate.

a1_12.text: Personal pronouns: I, you, he, she, it, we, they
a1_12.note: You always need an explicit subject - you can't skip I
a1_12.exp:
Unlike Russian, in Englishthe subject is always obligatory.
In Russian: “I’m going home.” In English: I am going home.

Object forms (after a verb or preposition): me, you, him, her, it, us, them.
mistakes: Is cold today → It is cold (the subject cannot be omitted)
Me like pizza → I like pizza
She told to him → She told him
ex: She is a teacher.
She's a teacher.
Tell him the truth.
Tell him the truth.
Can you help me?
Can you help me?

a1_13.text: Possessive adjectives: my, your, his, her, its, our, their
a1_13.note: my book, her car, their house - do not change by number
a1_13.exp:
Possessive adjectives show ownership and go before the noun.
Important: its (without apostrophe) = ownership; it's (with an apostrophe) = it is.

Do not change in gender and number: my friend / my friends - the same.
mistakes: Its a big house → It's a big house (it's = it is)
The cat hurt it's paw → hurt its paw (its = belonging)
Their friends are nice → Their friends are nice
ex: This is my phone.
This is my phone.
Their dog is cute.
Their dog is cute.
The cat hurt its paw.
The cat injured its paw.

a1_14.note: cats, boxes, knives, children, men, sheep
a1_14.exp:
Basic rule: add -s.

Special cases:
• -s, -ss, -sh, -ch, -x, -o: + es → boxes, watches, tomatoes
• vowel + -y: + s → boys, days
• consonant + -y: -y → ies → cities, babies
• -f/-fe: → ves → knives, leaves, wives

Irregular: child→children, man→men, woman→women, tooth→teeth, foot→feet, mouse→mice, person→people, fish→fish, sheep→sheep
mistakes: two childrens → two children
three men → three men
boxs → boxes (add -es)
ex: one bus → two buses
one bus → two buses
one child → many children
one child → many children

a1_15.exp:
this (this) - singular + next
these (these) - plural + nearby
that (that) - singular + far or already known
those (those) - plural + far

Also used about time: this week (this week), that year (that year).
mistakes: These is my pen → This is my pen
Those are that books → Those are those/the books
That book over here - this (near), that (far)
ex: This is my bag.
This is my bag.
Those shoes are expensive.
Those shoes are expensive.
What is that?
What is it?

a1_16.note: There is a park. There are five rooms.
a1_16.exp:
The construction there is/are means “exists/exist”, “is”.
• There is + singular noun. number
• There are + plural noun. number

Negation: There isn't / There aren't
Question: Is there...? / Are there...?

⚠️ Not to be confused with it is! There is a book on the table (the book exists there) vs It is a good book (characteristic).
ex: There is a cinema near here.
There is a cinema nearby.
Are there any shops nearby? — Yes, there are.
Are there any shops nearby? - Yes.

a1_17.text: Prepositions of place: in, on, at, under, next to, behind, between
a1_17.note: in the box, on the table, at the station
a1_17.exp:
Three main prepositions:
• in = inside something: in the box, in the city, in bed
• on = on the surface: on the table, on the wall, on the left
• at = at a specific point: at the station, at home, at school

Others: under (under), next to/beside (near), behind (behind), in front of (in front), between (between), opposite (opposite)
ex: The keys are on the table.
The keys are on the table.
She is in the kitchen.
She's in the kitchen.
Meet me at the entrance.
Meet me at the entrance.

a1_18.note: Open your books. Don't run. Please sit down.
a1_18.exp:
Imperative = base form of a verb without a subject.
Negation: Don't + infinitive
Please makes the request politer (at the beginning or end).

To include the speaker: Let's + infinitive → Let's go! Let's eat!
ex: Turn left at the crossroads.
Turn left at the intersection.
Don't touch that!
Don't touch this!
Let's have a break.
Let's take a break.

a1_19.note: I can swim. She can't drive. Can I help you?
a1_19.exp:
can is a modal verb, expresses:
1. Skill/Ability: I can play the guitar
2. Opportunity: It can be dangerous
3. Resolution (spoken): Can I use your phone?

⚠️ can never changes: he can (not he cans). After it there is an infinitive without to.
mistakes: Can you to help me? → Can you help (without to)
She cans do it → She can do it (can does not change)
<s>I can speak French</s> → I can speak French
ex: I can speak three languages.
I speak three languages.
She can't come today.
She can't come today.
Can you help me, please?
Could you help me?

a1_20.text: Question words: what, where, who, when, how, why, which, whose, how much/many
a1_20.note: What is this? Where do you live? How old are you?
a1_20.exp:
Question words appear at the beginning of the question, followed by an auxiliary verb before the subject.

• what = what/which
• where = where/where
• who = who (question without do: Who lives here?)
• when = when
• why = why
• how = how; how much = how many (uncountable); how many = how many (count); how old = how old; how long = how long
ex: Where does she work?
Where does she work?
What time is it?
What time is it?
How many brothers do you have?
How many brothers do you have?

a1_21.note: one, two, three... twenty-one, a hundred, a thousand
a1_21.exp:
Numerals 1–12: special words (one, two, three... twelve).
13–19: + -teen (thirteen, fourteen... nineteen; exceptions: thirteen, fifteen, eighteen).
Tens: twenty, thirty, forty, fifty, sixty, seventy, eighty, ninety.
Compounds: 21 = twenty-one (via hyphen).

100 = a/one hundred; 1000 = a/one thousand.
After hundred/thousand - and (BrE): two hundred and fifty.
ex: She is twenty-three years old.
She is twenty-three years old.
The ticket costs four hundred pounds.
The ticket costs four hundred pounds.

a1_22.note: the first, the second, the third, the fourth... the twenty-first
a1_22.exp:
Ordinal numbers are formed by adding -th: fourth, fifth, sixth...
Exceptions: first (1st), second (2nd), third (3rd), fifth (5th), eighth (8th), ninth (9th), twelfth (12th).
Always used with the article the: the first day, the third floor.

Fractions: ½ = a half, ⅓ = a third, ¼ = a quarter.
ex: My office is on the third floor.
My office is on the third floor.
Today is the first of March.
Today is the first of March.

a1_23.note: John's car, my sister's friend, the children's toys, the teachers' room
a1_23.exp:
In English, belonging is expressed by an apostrophe + s:
• Singular: Tom's book, the dog's tail
• Plural with -s: apostrophe only: the teachers' room, my parents' house
• Irregular plural: 's: the children's playground, men's clothes
• Names ending with -s: James's / James' - both options are correct
ex: This is Anna's laptop.
This is Anna's laptop.
The children's toys are in the box.
Children's toys in a box.

a1_24.text: Prepositions of movement: to, into, out of, up, down, along, across, through
a1_24.note: go to school, walk into the room, run across the street
a1_24.exp:
• to = direction to a point: go to work, walk to the park
• into = movement inward: come into the room, jump into the pool
• out of = movement outward: get out of the car, take out of the bag
• up/down = up/down: climb up the hill, walk down the stairs
• along = along: walk along the river
• across = across/through: swim across the lake, walk across the road
• through = through: drive through the tunnel
ex: She walked into the room.
She entered the room.
He ran across the street.
He ran across the street.
We drove through the forest.
We drove through the forest.

a2_01.exp:
Past Simple - a completed action in the past with or without a specific tense.

Rules for writing the ending -ed:
• Most verbs: + ed → worked, played
• End in -e: + d → loved, used
• Consonant + -y: -y → ied → studied, tried
• One vowel + one consonant (short stressed syllable): double the consonant → stopped, planned
a2_01.tip: Markers: yesterday, last week/year, ago, in 2010, at 5 o'clock.
mistakes: She is going to school every day → She goes (habit = Present Simple)
I am going to the cinema tomorrow - perhaps, but I'm going to see a film more precisely with intention
He is wanting → He wants (stative verb)

tags: in the morning (of a specific day)
markers.note: Key rule: specific time in the past → Past Simple, not Present Perfect!

ex: She worked all day yesterday.
She worked all day yesterday.
They played tennis last Sunday.
They played tennis last Sunday.

a2_02.exp:
About 200 irregular verbs need to be learned by heart. The most frequent 50 cover ~90% of uses.

Groups by similarity of changes:
• AAA (do not change): cut, put, hit, set, let
• ABA (return to the beginning): run→ran→run, come→came→come
• ABC (all different): go→went→gone, be→was/were→been
mistakes: She went → She went
I bought → I bought
They didn't go → They didn't go (after did - basic form)

markers.note: Question word when? always requires Past Simple (not Present Perfect).

ex: I went to Paris last summer.
I went to Paris last summer.
She saw a great film.
She watched a great movie.
We had a meeting at 9.
We had a meeting at 9.

a2_03.note: She didn't go (not: didn't go). I didn't see him.
a2_03.exp:
Negation: didn't (= did not) + infinitive for all persons.
⚠️ After didn't - base form of the verb (not past tense!): She didn't go (not went).
mistakes: I didn't go → I didn't go
She didn't come → She didn't come
Did she played? → Did she play?
She didn't come → She didn't come
What did he do? → What did he do?
ex: I didn't see him yesterday.
I didn't see him yesterday.
She didn't come to work.
She didn't show up for work.

a2_04.text: Past Simple - questions: Did you? Where did she go?
a2_04.note: Did they arrive? What did he say?
a2_04.exp:
Question: Did + subject + infinitive?
Special questions: Where/When/What + did + subject + infinitive?

⚠️ Exception: the question “Who performed the action?” is built without did: Who called? Who broke the vase?
mistakes: I will to go → I will go (without to)
<s>She wills come</s> → She will come (will does not change)
I think she go tomorrow → she will go

markers.note: will is used for spontaneous decisions, promises, predictions without evidence.

ex: Did you enjoy the film?
Did you like the movie?
Where did they go?
Where did they go?
Who told you that?
Who told you this?

a2_05.text: was / were - verb to be in the past
a2_05.exp:
Past tense of the verb to be:
• was - with I, he, she, it
• were - with you, we, they

Negation: wasn't / weren't
Question: Was she...? Were they...?
mistakes: I am going to will do - only one thing: I'm going to do
She is going to does → she is going to do
<s>He gonna do</s> - colloquial form, unacceptable in writing

markers.note: going to = a pre-made decision or prediction based on visible signs.

ex: I was very tired last night.
I was very tired last night.
They were at home all day.
They were at home all day.
Was it expensive?
Was it expensive?

a2_06.note: I'll call you back. It will rain tomorrow. I'll have the pasta.
a2_06.exp:
will is used for:
1. Spontaneous decisions (made at the time of speech): I'll help you with that.
2. Predictions without a specific plan: I think it will rain.
3. Promises: I won't tell anyone.
4. Requests: Will you open the window?

Abbreviation: 'll. Negation: won't (= will not).
mistakes: I will to go → I will go (without to)
<s>She wills come</s> → She will come (will does not change)
ex: The phone is ringing. — I'll get it!
The phone is ringing. - I will answer!
It will be cold tomorrow.
It will be cold tomorrow.

a2_07.text: going to - intentions and obvious predictions
a2_07.note: I'm going to visit Paris. Look out - you're going to fall!
a2_07.exp:
going to is used for:
1. Pre-made decisions and intentions: I'm going to start a diet next week.
2. Predictions with visible signs: Look at those clouds - it's going to rain!

Form: am/is/are + going to + infinitive
a2_07.exc: will vs going to: "I'll have coffee" - I’m deciding right now; "I'm going to have coffee" - I already decided earlier.
mistakes: She is going to does → She is going to do
<s>I am going to will do</s> - you can’t have two futures
ex: I'm going to study medicine.
I'm going to study medicine.
She's going to have a baby.
She's expecting a baby.

a2_08.note: She is reading now. They are playing outside.
a2_08.exp:
Present Continuous is an action taking place right now or temporarily during this period.

Form: am/is/are + verb + -ing

Rules for writing -ing:
• Most: just + ing → working, playing
• Ends with -e: remove -e + ing → making, coming
• Short stressed syllable: double the last consonant → running, sitting
a2_08.tip: Markers: now, at the moment, currently, this week/month.
mistakes: I was visit → I was visiting
She were working → She was working (she/he/it → was)
At 8 I was work → I was working

markers.note: These markers indicate action right now or a temporary situation.

ex: I am studying English right now.
I'm studying English now.
She's working from home this month.
She is working from home this month.

a2_09.note: I'm meeting Tom tomorrow. We're flying to Rome on Friday.
a2_09.exp:
Present Continuous is used for specific agreements in the future - when the time and place have already been determined.

Difference:
• I'm meeting Alice tomorrow - the meeting has been agreed (a specific plan)
• I'll meet Alice tomorrow - intention or proposal
mistakes: I'm meeting Tom in next week → meeting Tom next week (without in)
We are flying to Rome on the Friday → on Friday

markers.note: Present Continuous for the future - specific agreements, there is a time and place.

ex: I'm having dinner with Alex tonight.
I'm having dinner with Alex tonight.
They're getting married in June.
They are getting married in June.

a2_10.text: Present Simple vs Present Continuous - difference
a2_10.note: I drink coffee (habit) vs I am drinking coffee (right now)
a2_10.exp:
Present Simple: habits, facts, schedules, general truths.
Present Continuous: something that is happening right now or temporarily.

Compare:
• She speaks French. - knows the language (always)
• She is speaking French. - speaks right now

⚠️ Stative verbs (know, love, want, understand...) - only Present Simple!
mistakes: I am drink coffee every morning → I drink (habit)
She knowing French → She knows French
Water is boiling at 100°C → Water boils (fact)

markers.note: The marker determines the choice: now/at the moment → Continuous; always/every day → Simple.

ex: Water boils at 100°C. (факт)
Water boils at 100°C.
I'm reading a great book this week. (временно)
I'm reading a great book this week.

a2_11.note: You should see a doctor. You shouldn't eat so much.
a2_11.exp:
should expresses mild advice or personal opinion. Weaker than must.
After should - infinitive without to.

Also used for criticism or regret: You should have called (should have called).
mistakes: You should to see a doctor → should see (without to)
She should go → She should go
I should have gone → I should have gone
ex: You should exercise more.
You should exercise more.
She shouldn't work so hard.
She shouldn't work so hard.

a2_12.text: must / mustn't - obligation and strict prohibition
a2_12.note: You must wear a seatbelt. You mustn't smoke here.
a2_12.exp:
must - a strong obligation (often internal) or a categorical requirement.
mustn't - a strict prohibition (absolutely impossible!).

⚠️ Important difference: mustn't ≠ don't have to!
• mustn't = prohibited
• don't have to = not required, but possible
mistakes: You mustn't to smoke → mustn't smoke
I must to do this → I must do this (without to)
You mustn't come ≠ “you don’t need” is “you can’t!” For "don't have to": don't have to
ex: You must show your passport.
You must present your passport.
You mustn't smoke here.
You can't smoke here.

a2_13.note: I have to work late. She doesn't have to come.
a2_13.exp:
have to - a necessity due to external rules, circumstances or other people's requirements.

Difference from must:
• must - internal necessity: I must call her (I myself consider it necessary)
• have to - external: I have to wear a uniform (rule)

don't have to=optional (not necessary, but allowed).
mistakes: <s>I have to can do this</s> - you can’t have two modals in a row
She doesn't have to don't come → She doesn't have to come
<s>You must not come</s> → mustn't come
ex: I have to finish this report by Friday.
I have to submit my report by Friday.
You don't have to come if you don't want to.
You don't have to come.

a2_14.text: could - past skill and polite requests
a2_14.note: She could swim when she was 5. Could you help me?
a2_14.exp:
could is the past tense of can. Two main meanings:
1. Skill in the past: I could read at 4 years old.
2. Polite requests (more polite than can): Could you pass the salt, please?

Also for present possibility: It could be true.
mistakes: She could to swim → She could swim (without to)
Can I when I was a child → I could when I was a child
<s>Could you speak slowly?</s> → Could you speak slowly?
ex: She could play the violin as a child.
She knew how to play the violin as a child.
Could you speak more slowly, please?
Could you speak more slowly?

a2_15.exp:
Monosyllabic and disyllabic in -y: + er (fast→faster, happy→happier).
Polysyllabic: more + adjective.

Writing rules: -e → + r (nice→nicer); short stressed syllable: double (big→bigger); -y → ier (heavy→heavier).

Irregular: good→better, bad→worse, far→further/farther, much/many→more
After the comparative - than: She is taller than her sister.
mistakes: She is more tall than → She is taller than
He is more better → He is better
She is tallest in class → She is the tallest
ex: This film is more interesting than that one.
This movie is more interesting than that.
Today is worse than yesterday.
Today is worse than yesterday.

a2_16.note: the biggest, the most beautiful, the best
a2_16.exp:
Monosyllabic: the + -est. Polysyllabic: the most.
The writing rules are the same as for the comparative degree.

Irregular: good→the best, bad→the worst, far→the furthest, much/many→the most

⚠️ Always with the article the! After superlatives, often in or of: the tallest girl in the class.
mistakes: He is most clever → He is the most clever / the cleverest
She is the most best → She is the best
This is the most interesting film → the most interesting film (need the)
ex: This is the most expensive restaurant in the city.
This is the most expensive restaurant in the city.
He is the best player on the team.
He is the best player on the team.

a2_17.note: I have some money. Do you have any? I don't have any.
a2_17.exp:
some - in affirmative sentences and in offers/requests.
any - in questions and negations.

Exception: some in questions when we expect an affirmative answer or offer something: Would you like some tea? Can I have some water?
mistakes: I have some money? (question) → Do I have any money?
I don't have some cash → I don't have any cash
Would you like any tea? → Would you like some tea? (offer)
ex: I bought some bread and some milk.
I bought bread and milk.
Is there any milk in the fridge?
Is there milk in the refrigerator?
I don't have any cash on me.
I don't have cash with me.

a2_18.text: much / many / a lot of / a few / a little
a2_18.note: much water (uncountable), many people (countable), a lot of - everywhere
a2_18.exp:
much - with uncountables (much water, much time, much money).
many - with countables (many people, many books, many times).
a lot of / lots of - with both, conversational style.

a few - a little (countable, neutral/positive).
few - few, almost none (countable, negative).
a little - a little (uncountable, neutral).
little - little, almost none (uncountable, negative).
mistakes: Much people → Many people (countable)
Many money → Much money (uncountable)
A few water → A little water
ex: I don't have much time.
I don't have much time.
She has a few friends in London.
She has several friends in London.
There is a little sugar left.
There is some sugar left.

a2_19.note: water, information, advice - not allowed: a water, two advices
a2_19.exp:
Countable: you can count, there are plural. number: a book, two books.
Uncountable: cannot be counted directly - no plural. numbers, no indefinite article.

Typical uncountables: water, milk, bread, rice, money, information, advice, news, weather, luggage, furniture, hair, music, work

To indicate portions: a glass of water, a piece of advice, a loaf of bread, a bag of rice
mistakes: Two advices → Two pieces of advice (advice uncountable)
An information → Some information
Three furnitures → Three pieces of furniture
ex: Can I have some information, please?
May I know something?
She gave me very useful advice.
She gave me very useful advice.

a2_20.text: Prepositions in / on / at for time
a2_20.note: in March, in 2024, in winter; on Monday, on June 5; at 3pm, at night
a2_20.exp:
at → exact time: at 6 o'clock, at noon, at midnight, at night, at the weekend (BrE)
on → days and dates: on Monday, on 5 March, on my birthday, on New Year's Day
in → periods: in July, in 2023, in the morning/afternoon/evening, in summer, in the 21st century

Without preposition: this/last/next + time: this morning, last week, next year
mistakes: at Monday → on Monday
in 8 o'clock → at 8 o'clock
on the morning → in the morning
in my birthday → on my birthday
ex: The meeting is at half past three.
Meeting at half past four.
She was born on the 12th of April.
She was born on April 12.
I started this job in October.
I started this work in October.

a2_21.text: for / since / ago - duration and beginning
a2_21.note: for three years, since 2020, three years ago
a2_21.exp:
for - duration (how long): for two hours, for a week, for years. Used with different tenses.
since - initial moment (since): since Monday, since 2019, since I was a child. Used with the Present Perfect.
ago - time ago from now: three days ago, a month ago. Only with Past Simple.
mistakes: <s>I've been here since three years</s> → for three years
I saw him since Monday → I saw him on Monday (ago with Past Simple, not since!)
Three days before → Three days ago
ex: I've been here for six months.
I've been here for six months now.
She's worked here since 2020.
She has been working here since 2020.
I saw him two days ago.
I saw him two days ago.

a2_22.note: It's cold, isn't it? You like jazz, don't you?
a2_22.exp:
Tag questions are used to confirm or clarify.

Rule: affirmative sentence → negative tag, and vice versa.
Auxiliary verb in the tail = tense of the main sentence.

Intonation: ↘ (falling) = confirmation; ↗ (rising) = real question.
mistakes: It's cold, isn't he? → isn't it
She can swim, can't he? → can't she
You don't like it, don't you? → do you? (negative sentence → positive tail)
ex: It's a nice day, isn't it?
Nice day, right?
You don't like horror films, do you?
You don't like horror films, do you?
She can swim, can't she?
She can swim, can't she?

a2_23.note: I've got a car. Have you got a pen? She hasn't got time.
a2_23.exp:
have got is a colloquial British form of possession. Value = have.

• Affirmation: I have got / I've got a laptop.
• Denial: I haven't got / I don't have
• Question: Have you got...? / Do you have...?

⚠️ have got is used only in the present. For the past - only had.
mistakes: I've got a car in 2010 → I bought a car in 2010 (have got only the present)
She had got a dog → She had a dog (past - only had)
ex: I've got two brothers.
I have two brothers.
Have you got the time?
Don't you know what time it is?
She hasn't got any cash.
She doesn't have cash.

a2_24.text: Adverbs of manner: quickly, carefully, well, hard, fast
a2_24.note: She sings beautifully. He works hard. She drives fast.
a2_24.exp:
Most adverbs are formed from adjectives + -ly: quick → quickly, careful → carefully, slow → slowly.

Special cases:
• good → well (not goodly)
• fast → fast (not fastly)
• hard → hard (not hardly - this is another word: “barely”)
• late → late (not lately - this is “lately”)

Place: usually after the verb/object: She speaks English well.
mistakes: She sings beautiful → She sings beautifully
He works hardly → He works hard (hardly = barely)
She drives fastly → She drives fast
ex: He explained it clearly.
He explained it clearly.
She ran fast.
She ran fast.
They worked hard all day.
They worked hard all day.

a2_25.text: Adverbs of frequency and their place in a sentence
a2_25.note: always, usually, often, sometimes, rarely, never - before the main verb
a2_25.exp:
Adverbs of frequency: always, usually, often, sometimes, occasionally, rarely, rarely, never.

Place in a sentence:
• Before the main verb: She always drinks tea.
• After the verb to be: He is always late.
• After the auxiliary verb: She has never been to Italy.

Also: every day/week, once a week, twice a month - at the end of the sentence.
mistakes: She always is late → She is always late (after to be)
He drinks always coffee → He always drinks coffee (before the main verb)
<s>She has never been always on time</s> - two adverbs together are inappropriate
ex: I usually wake up at 7.
I usually wake up at 7.
She is never late for work.
She is never late for work.
They meet once a week.
They meet once a week.

a2_26.note: a lovely small old red Italian leather bag
a2_26.exp:
When several adjectives appear before a noun, a strict order is observed: Opinion → Size → Age → Shape → Color → Origin → Material → Purpose
(Opinion → Size → Age → Shape → Color → Origin → Material → Purpose)

Example: a small beautiful old square brown French wooden writing desk
mistakes: <s>a wooden French old small bag</s> → order: size → age → origin → material
a beautiful Italian big car → big beautiful Italian car (size before opinion - no)
ex: a lovely little old cottage
lovely little old cottage
a big red Italian sports car
big red italian sports car

b1_01.note: I have seen it. She has gone. They have arrived.
b1_01.exp:
Present Perfect = have/has + V3 (third form of the verb).

Three main meanings:
1. Life Experience (no time specified): I have visited Tokyo.
2. Result in the present: I have lost my keys. (there are no keys now)
3. Unfinished action, ongoing now: She has lived here for 5 years.

V3 regular verbs = Past Simple (worked, played). Incorrect ones need to be known separately.
b1_01.tip: Markers: ever, never, already, yet, just, recently, lately, so far.
mistakes: I have seen him yesterday → I saw him yesterday (yesterday = Past Simple!)
She has gone → She has gone
I have been to Rome last year → I went to Rome last year

markers.note: Never used with a specific time of the past (yesterday, last year, in 2020 → Past Simple).

ex: Have you ever eaten sushi?
Have you ever eaten sushi?
I've just finished my homework.
I just finished my homework.
She hasn't called yet.
She hasn't called yet.

b1_02.text: ever / never / already / yet / just - Present Perfect markers
b1_02.note: Have you ever...? I've never... I've already... Not yet. I've just...
b1_02.exp:
ever - someday (in questions about experience). Location: in front of V3.
never - never (negative value). Location: in front of V3.
already - already (earlier than expected). Place: before V3 or at the end.
yet - already/yet. In questions (Have you finished yet?) and negations (I haven't finished yet). Place: end of sentence.
just - just now. Location: in front of V3.
mistakes: Have you ever been to Paris yet? → ever in the question, yet at the end
I've already not finished → I haven't finished yet
I just have eaten → I have just eaten (just before V3)

markers.note: ever/never - before V3; already - before V3 or at the end; yet - only at the end.

ex: Have you ever been to Scotland?
Have you been to Scotland?
I've never eaten snails.
I've never eaten snails.
I've already seen that film.
I've already seen this film.
She hasn't replied yet.
She hasn't answered yet.

b1_03.text: Present Perfect vs Past Simple - the key difference
b1_03.note: I've been to Paris (experience) vs I went to Paris in 2019 (specific time)
b1_03.exp:
This is one of the most important differences in English grammar.

Present Perfect - connection with the present, time is NOT indicated:
I've lost my wallet. (no wallet now - this is important)

Past Simple - completed past, time is indicated or implied:
I lost my wallet yesterday. (yesterday is a specific time)

⚠️ With specific time markers (yesterday, last week, in 2020, ago) → Past Simple only!
mistakes: I have seen him last week → I saw him last week (specific time = Past Simple)
Did you ever eat sushi? → Have did you ever eat sushi?
I met him and he has told me → he told me (in one context of the past)

markers.note: If there is a specific time (yesterday, in 2020, last year) → Past Simple only.

ex: I've met the new director. (он сейчас здесь, это актуально)
I met the new director.
I met him last Tuesday. (конкретное время)
I met him last Tuesday.

b1_04.text: Present Perfect with for and since
b1_04.note: I've lived here for 5 years / since 2019.
b1_04.exp:
for - duration: for two days, for a year, for a long time, for ages
since - starting point: since Monday, since I was a child, since 2015

The question “How long?” — How long + Present Perfect: How long have you known her?
mistakes: I've lived here since five years → for five years
She's known him for 2019 → she's known him since 2019
How long did you live here? → How long have you lived here? (ongoing now)

markers.note: for + time period; since + starting point. Both are used with the Present Perfect (Simple and Continuous).

ex: She's worked here for ten years.
She's been working here for ten years.
I've known him since university.
I've known him since university.

b1_05.text: Present Perfect Continuous: have been + V-ing
b1_05.note: I have been waiting for an hour. She has been studying all day.
b1_05.exp:
Present Perfect Continuous = have/has been + V-ing

Emphasis on duration or incompleteness of action. Answers the question “How long?”
Often explains the visible result in the present: You look tired - have you been running?

Difference with Present Perfect Simple:
• I've read 50 pages. - result, completeness
• I've been reading all evening. - emphasis on the process
mistakes: I've been learning since three hours → for three hours
I've been read all evening → I've been reading
<s>I've been know her for years</s> → know - stative verb, use Present Perfect Simple

markers.note: The Present Perfect Continuous emphasizes the duration rather than the result.

ex: I've been learning English for two years.
I've been learning English for two years now.
Why are your hands dirty? — I've been fixing the car.
Why are your hands dirty? — I was repairing the car.

b1_06.note: I was reading at 8pm. They were talking all evening.
b1_06.exp:
Past Continuous = was/were + V-ing

Values:
1. Action in process at a specific moment in the past: At 9pm I was having dinner.
2. Background activity interrupted by another: I was walking when it started to rain.
3. Parallel actions: While she was cooking, he was watching TV.
mistakes: She was walk → She was walking
They were talking → They were talking
I was work at 9pm → I was working

markers.note: while/as → background (Past Continuous); when → event (Past Simple).

ex: It was raining when I left home.
It was raining when I left the house.
What were you doing at 7 yesterday?
What were you doing yesterday at 7?

b1_07.text: Past Simple vs Past Continuous - background and event
b1_07.note: While I was cooking, he called. I was walking when it started to rain.
b1_07.exp:
Typical design: long background action (Past Continuous) + short event (Past Simple).

Unions:
• when - used with the Past Simple for the event: She was sleeping when the alarm went off.
• while/as - used with Past Continuous for background: While I was watching TV, the power went out.
mistakes: While I cooked, he called → While I was cooking, he called (background = Continuous)
I was walking when it was starting to rain → when it started (event = Past Simple)

markers.note: while + Past Continuous (background) + when + Past Simple (event).

ex: She was having a bath when the phone rang.
She was taking a bath when the phone rang.
While he was giving his speech, someone fell asleep.
While he was making his speech, someone fell asleep.

b1_08.text: Type 0: If + Present, Present - facts and laws
b1_08.note: If you heat water to 100°C, it boils.
b1_08.exp:
Type zero - for scientific facts, laws of nature, general truths. Both actions always happen together.

Form: If + Present Simple, Present Simple
Instead of if you can use when: When you mix red and blue, you get purple.
mistakes: If you heat water, it will boil → it boils (zero type - Present + Present)
When you mix them, you will get → you get
ex: If you heat ice, it melts.
If you heat ice, it melts.
If it rains, the streets get wet.
If it rains, the streets get wet.

b1_09.text: 1st type: If + Present Simple, will - real future
b1_09.note: If it rains tomorrow, I'll stay home.
b1_09.exp:
A real, probable situation in the future.

Form: If + Present Simple, will + infinitive
⚠️ In the if-part - NEVER Future! Only Present Simple.

Instead of will in the main part you can: can, may, might, should.
Parts can be changed: I'll stay home if it rains. (without comma)
mistakes: If it will rain → If it rains (there is NO will in the if part!)
If she will study hard, she passes → If she studies, she will pass
ex: If she studies hard, she'll pass the exam.
If she studies well, she will pass the exam.
If you need help, I can come.
If you need help, I can come.

b1_10.text: Type 2: If + Past Simple, would - unreal present
b1_10.note: If I had a car, I would drive to work. If I were you...
b1_10.exp:
An unreal or unlikely situation in the present or future.

Form: If + Past Simple, would + infinitive
⚠️ The verb in the if-part is Past Simple, but the meaning is present/future!
⚠️ were is used for all persons: If I were you, If she were here is a formal language standard.
mistakes: If I would have a car → If I had a car
If I was you → If I were you (formally)
I would can do it → I could do it (you can’t have two modals)
ex: If I won the lottery, I would travel the world.
If I won the lottery, I would travel the world.
If I were taller, I'd play basketball.
If I were taller, I would play basketball.

b1_11.text: Type 1 vs Type 2: real vs unlikely
b1_11.note: If I win (1st, really) vs If I won (2nd, unlikely)
b1_11.exp:
The choice of type reflectsyour confidencein the reality of the situation.

• If I see her (1st type) - I expect to see her
• If I saw her (2nd type) - unlikely or I’m just fantasizing

This is not a difference in grammar, but a difference in the attitude of the speaker.
mistakes: Type 1 and 2 confusion: If I win (real) vs If I won (unlikely)
If I won, I will buy → cannot be mixed: would buy
ex: If it rains tomorrow, I'll take an umbrella. (реально возможно)
If it rains tomorrow, I will take an umbrella.
If it rained every day, I'd move to Spain. (маловероятная фантазия)
If it rained every day, I would move to Spain.

b1_12.text: Passive Voice - Present Simple: is/are + V3
b1_12.note: Coffee is grown in Brazil. The car is made in Germany.
b1_12.exp:
Passive voice is used when:
• The action itself is important, not the person who performs it
• Performer unknown or obvious

Form Present Simple Passive: am/is/are + V3
The performer (if needed) is added with by: The window was broken by the ball.
mistakes: Coffee is grown in Brazil → Coffee is grown
The letter written in French → The letter is written
English spoke here → English is spoken
ex: English is spoken in many countries.
English is spoken in many countries.
The letter is written in French.
The letter is written in French.

b1_13.text: Passive Voice - Past Simple: was/were + V3
b1_13.note: The bridge was built in 1890. They were arrested.
b1_13.exp:
Past Simple Passive: was/were + V3
• was - units. number
• were - plural. number

Active → Passive: Someone stole my car. → My car was stolen.
mistakes: The bridge was built in 1890 → was built
They were arrested → They were arrested
The car stolen → The car was stolen
ex: The Eiffel Tower was built in 1889.
The Eiffel Tower was built in 1889.
Three people were injured in the accident.
Three people were injured in the accident.

b1_14.text: Passive Voice - Present Perfect: has/have been + V3
b1_14.note: The car has been repaired. All invitations have been sent.
b1_14.exp:
Present Perfect Passive: has/have been + V3

Used when the result in the present is important, and not when exactly it happened.
mistakes: The project has been complete → has been completed
It have been sent → It has been sent
All guests have been informed → have been informed
ex: The project has been completed.
The project is completed.
All the guests have been informed.
All guests have been notified.

b1_15.note: "I'm tired." → He said he was tired.
b1_15.exp:
When translated into indirect speech, the tenses are “shifted” back:
• Present Simple → Past Simple: "I work" → he said he worked
• Past Simple → Past Perfect: "I went" → she said she had gone
• Present Perfect → Past Perfect: "I've seen" → he said he had seen
• will → would; can → could; is → was; am going → was going
mistakes: He said he is tired → He said he was tired (time shift)
She told that she was late → She told me/us/him that... (tell requires an object)
He said me that → He told me that / He said that
ex: She said: 'I am leaving.' → She said she was leaving.
She said she was leaving.
He told me: 'I can't come.' → He told me he couldn't come.
He said he couldn't come.

b1_16.note: "Where do you live?" → She asked where I lived.
b1_16.exp:
In indirect questions:
1. No inversion (word order as in the statement)
2. No auxiliary do/did
3. General questions (Yes/No) → if/whether + subject + verb
mistakes: She asked where do I live → she asked where I lived (no inversion!)
He asked me what was I doing → what I was doing
She asked if was I tired → if I was tired
ex: "Where do you work?" → She asked where I worked.
She asked where I worked.
"Are you married?" → He wanted to know if I was married.
He wanted to know if I was married.

b1_17.text: say vs tell in indirect speech
b1_17.note: He said that... / He told me that... - after tell you need a face!
b1_17.exp:
say - without the required addition: She said she was tired.
tell - ALWAYS with an addition (to whom it is told): She told me she was tired.

❌ He told that he was late. ✓ He said that he was late.
mistakes: He told that he was late → He said that / He told me that
She said me the news → She told me the news
They told about the problem → They talked about it / said something about it
ex: She said she needed help.
She said she needed help.
He told us the meeting was cancelled.
He informed us that the meeting was cancelled.

b1_18.note: I enjoy reading. She finished writing. He avoided making mistakes.
b1_18.exp:
These verbs require a gerund (V-ing) after them:
enjoy, finish, avoid, mind, suggest, keep, consider, deny, imagine, miss, practice, risk, admit, delay, dislike, fancy, give up, involve, put off, recommend, resist

Memorization trick: if you can replace it with “process”, it’s most likely a gerund.
mistakes: I enjoy to swim → I enjoy swimming
She finished to write → She finished writing
He avoided to make → He avoided making
ex: I enjoy swimming in the sea.
I like to swim in the sea.
She's considering moving abroad.
She is thinking about moving abroad.
He avoided making eye contact.
He avoided eye contact.

b1_19.note: I want to go. She decided to stay. He managed to finish.
b1_19.exp:
These verbs require an infinitive with to:
want, decide, hope, plan, manage, agree, promise, refuse, fail, expect, offer, learn, need, afford, arrange, attempt, choose, claim, demand, deserve, help, pretend, tend, threaten
mistakes: She decided going → She decided to go
He wants going out → He wants to go out
I hope seeing you → I hope to see you
ex: She decided to quit her job.
She decided to quit.
I hope to see you soon.
Hope to see you soon.
He failed to answer.
He couldn't answer.

b1_20.text: Verbs with both (like, love, hate, start, begin)
b1_20.note: I like reading = I like to read - slight difference
b1_20.exp:
After like/love/hate/prefer both options are possible:
• V-ing - we talk about the action as a whole: I love cooking. (I generally like to cook)
• to-inf - we are talking about a specific case: I'd like to cook tonight.

After start/begin/continue/cease there is practically no difference.
mistakes: I'd like cooking tonight → I'd like to cook tonight (specific case)
She started to working → She started working or to work
ex: I love travelling / to travel.
I like to travel.
She started working / to work here in May.
She started working here in May.

b1_21.text: used to - past habits and states
b1_21.note: I used to play football. She used to have long hair.
b1_21.exp:
used to - regular actions or conditions in the past that have already stopped.

Form: used to + infinitive
Denial: didn't use to
Question: Did you use to...?

⚠️ There is no present tense form! You can't: I use to for the present.
Difference from be used to: I am used to waking up early = I'm used to it (now).
mistakes: I use to play football → I used to play
Did you use to...? → Did you use to...? (after did - base form)
I used to living there → I used to live (infinitive without to+ing)
ex: I used to smoke, but I stopped.
I used to smoke, but I quit.
Did you use to play an instrument?
Have you played an instrument before?

b1_22.text: Relative clauses: who, which, that, where, whose
b1_22.note: The man who called. The book that I read. The place where we met.
b1_22.exp:
who - for people: The woman who called is my sister.
which - for objects and animals: The book which I borrowed was great.
that - for people and objects (in defining clauses): The car that he bought is new.
where - for places: The café where we met is closed.
whose - accessory: The girl whose bag was stolen...

In colloquial speech, a pronoun is often omitted if it is an object: The film (that) I saw...
mistakes: The man which called → the man who called (who for people)
The place who we met → the place where we met
The girl who's bag → the girl whose bag
ex: The man who lives next door is very friendly.
The man who lives next door is very friendly.
The hotel where we stayed had a pool.
The hotel we stayed in had a swimming pool.

b1_23.text: Conjunctions of contrast: although, however, despite, in spite of, whereas
b1_23.note: Although it was raining, we went out. Despite the rain, we went.
b1_23.exp:
although / even though / though + subordinate clause.
despite / in spite of + noun / gerund (NOT subordinate clause!).
however + new sentence (after period or semicolon).
whereas is a contrast between two facts.
mistakes: Despite it was raining → Despite the rain / Despite raining (noun/gerund!)
Although the rain, we went → Although it was raining (although + sentence)
ex: Although she was tired, she kept working.
Although she was tired, she continued to work.
Despite the rain, he cycled to work.
Despite the rain, he rode his bike to work.
It was expensive. However, it was worth it.
It was expensive. Still, it was worth it.

b1_24.note: I'll be working at 9 tomorrow. She'll be flying over the Atlantic.
b1_24.exp:
Future Continuous = will be + V-ing

Values:
1. An action that will be in progress at a specific moment in the future: At this time tomorrow, I'll be lying on the beach.
2. Planned, expected action in the future (natural course of events): I'll be seeing her tomorrow anyway.
3. Polite questions about plans (no pressure): Will you be coming to the party?
mistakes: I'll be work at 9 → I'll be working
She will working → She will be working

markers.note: Often used for polite questions about plans: Will you be using the car?

ex: Don't call at 8 — I'll be having dinner.
Don't call at 8 - I'll have dinner.
This time next week I'll be sitting on a beach.
This time next week I'll be sitting on the beach.

b1_25.note: By Friday I will have finished the report. By 2030 they will have built the bridge.
b1_25.exp:
Future Perfect = will have + V3

An action that will be completed before a certain point in the future.
Often used with by (the time), before: By the time you arrive, I will have cooked dinner.
mistakes: By Friday I will finish → I will have finished (will be completed by the deadline = Future Perfect)
I'll have finished by Sunday → I'll have finished

tags: by the end of the week
by the end of the year
markers.note: by (the time) is a key marker: the action will complete BEFORE this moment.

ex: I'll have finished this book by Sunday.
I will finish reading this book by Sunday.
By 2050, scientists will have found a cure.
By 2050, scientists will find a cure.

b1_26.text: Subordinate goals: to, in order to, so that, so as to
b1_26.note: I study hard to pass. She left early so that she could catch the train.
b1_26.exp:
to / in order to / so as to + infinitive - goal. In order to and so as to are a little more formal.
so that + subject + verb - purpose with another subject or with a modal.

⚠️ Denial: in order not to / so as not to (not to not).
mistakes: I study hard for passing → I study hard to pass
She left early for that she could catch the train → so that she could
In order to not upset him → in order not to upset him
ex: She studies hard in order to get a scholarship.
She studies hard to get a scholarship.
He left early so that he could catch the last train.
He left early to catch the last train.

b1_27.note: give up, look up, turn off, find out, get on, put off, carry on
b1_27.exp:
Phrasal verbs = verb + particle (preposition or adverb). The meaning is often idiomatic.

The most frequent:
• give up = give up, give up
• find out = find out, find out
• turn on/off = turn on/off
• look up = look up (in the dictionary/Internet)
• look after = take care
• put off = put off
• carry on = continue
• get on/along = to get along (with someone)
• bring up = to educate; bring up the topic
• come across = stumble upon
mistakes: She gave up to smoke → She gave up smoking
I looked up the word in → I looked it up (pronoun between verb and particle)
ex: I've given up smoking.
I quit smoking.
Can you look after my cat while I'm away?
Can you look after my cat while I'm away?
We need to find out what happened.
We need to find out what happened.

b2_01.note: By the time she arrived, he had already left.
b2_01.exp:
Past Perfect - an action that completed before another moment or event in the past.

Form: had + V3 for all faces.
Negation: hadn't + V3
Question: Had + subject + V3?

Typical conjunctions: before, after, when, by the time, already, just, never
mistakes: When I arrived, she already left → she had already left
He had never see snow → He had never seen snow
I had went → I had gone

markers.note: The Past Perfect shows that one past action happened BEFORE another.

ex: When I arrived, she had already left.
When I arrived, she had already left.
He had never seen snow before that winter.
He had never seen snow before that winter.

b2_02.text: Past Perfect Continuous: had been + V-ing
b2_02.note: He had been waiting for two hours when she arrived.
b2_02.exp:
Past Perfect Continuous = had been + V-ing
Emphasis on the duration of an action that occurred before another point in the past.
Often explains a cause or visible result in the past.
mistakes: She was exhausted — she had been work → she had been working
How long had you waited? → How long had you been waiting?

tags: for two hours when she arrived
markers.note: Emphasis on duration until a moment in the past. Often explains the cause of the condition.

ex: She was exhausted — she'd been working all night.
She was exhausted - she had worked all night.
How long had you been waiting before she arrived?
How long did you wait before she arrived?

b2_03.text: must have + V3 - confident conclusion about the past
b2_03.note: She must have forgotten. - I'm sure this is the only explanation
b2_03.exp:
must have + V3 = I'm sure this happened (the only logical explanation).

Confidence scale:
• must have done - almost certainly happened
• should have done - it was expected to happen
• may/might have done - it may have happened
• can't have done - it probably didn't happen
mistakes: She must forget → She must have forgotten
He must have leave early → He must have left
You must be exhausted yesterday - for the past: must have been
ex: You must have been exhausted after that journey.
You must be very tired after this trip.
She must have left early — her coat is gone.
She apparently left early - her coat was missing.

b2_04.text: can't have + V3 - impossibility in the past
b2_04.note: She can't have said that! He can't have been there.
b2_04.exp:
can't have + V3 = I'm sure this didn't happen (it's impossible).
The opposite of must have done.
mistakes: She can't forget that → She can't have forgotten
<s>He couldn't have been there</s> - that's right! can't have = impossibility; couldn't have = opportunity
ex: He can't have seen her — she was abroad.
He couldn't see her - she was abroad.
That can't have been the right address.
This could not be the correct address.

b2_05.text: should have + V3 - reproach and regret
b2_05.note: I should have called. You shouldn't have said that.
b2_05.exp:
should have + V3 = it would have been right to do it, but I didn’t (regret, reproach).
shouldn't have + V3 = shouldn't have done.

This is a very common construction for expressing regret and criticism.
mistakes: I should called her → I should have called
She shouldn't have went → She shouldn't have gone
You should have study → You should have studied
ex: I should have brought an umbrella.
I should have taken an umbrella.
She shouldn't have told him the secret.
She shouldn't have told him the secret.

b2_06.text: might / could have + V3 - possibility in the past
b2_06.note: She might have forgotten. It could have been worse.
b2_06.exp:
might/could have + V3 = this may have happened (we don't know for sure).

Also: could have done = could have done (but didn’t): I could have won if I had tried harder.
mistakes: She might forgot → She might have forgotten
He could have win → He could have won
ex: She might have forgotten about the meeting.
Perhaps she forgot about the meeting.
He could have left through the back door.
He could have gone out the back door.

b2_07.text: 3rd type of conditional: If + Past Perfect, would have + V3
b2_07.note: If I had studied harder, I would have passed.
b2_07.exp:
An unreal situation in the past - we are talking about something that did not happen.

Form: If + Past Perfect, would have + V3

Both elements are unreal:
• Condition not met
• There was no result either
mistakes: If I studied harder, I would have passed → If I had studied... (3rd type - Past Perfect!)
If she had taken medicine, she would recovered → she would have recovered
ex: If she had taken the medicine, she would have recovered.
If she took the medicine, she would get better.
If he hadn't left early, he would have met her.
If he hadn't left early, he would have met her.

b2_08.note: If I had studied, I would be fluent now. (past → present)
b2_08.exp:
Mixed conditionals connect different time plans:

1. Past condition → present result:
If + Past Perfect, would + infinitive
If I had taken that job, I would be in New York now.

2. Present condition → past result:
If + Past Simple, would have + V3
If she were more careful, she wouldn't have broken it.
mistakes: <s>If I had studied, I would be fluent yesterday</s> - mixed type about the past→present, not the past
If she were careful, she didn't break it → she wouldn't have broken it
ex: If I had studied medicine, I would be a doctor now.
If I had studied to become a doctor, I would be a doctor now.

b2_09.text: Future Passive and Passive with modals
b2_09.note: It will be done. It must be fixed. It should be checked.
b2_09.exp:
Modal + Passive: modal + be + V3
• will be done - will be done
• must be done - must be done
• should be done - should be done
• can be done - can be done
mistakes: The report will be published → will be published
It must be done → It must be done
It should be do → It should be done
ex: The report will be published tomorrow.
The report will be published tomorrow.
This mistake must be corrected immediately.
This error must be corrected immediately.

b2_10.note: I had my hair cut. She got her car fixed.
b2_10.exp:
have/get + object + V3 - you order a service or someone does something for you.

Compare:
• I cut my hair. - I cut my hair myself (which is not typical)
• I had my hair cut. - cut by a hairdresser

get is a little more conversational, have is a little more formal.
mistakes: I cut my hair at the hairdresser → I had my hair cut
She got her car to fix → She got her car fixed
ex: I need to have my teeth checked.
I need to have my teeth checked by a doctor.
She had her house painted last spring.
Last spring she painted the house (hiring workers).

b2_11.text: Passive reporting verbs: It is said that... / He is believed to...
b2_11.note: It is thought that prices will rise. She is known to be honest.
b2_11.exp:
Two construction options with the verbs say, think, believe, report, know, expect, consider:

1. It + passive + that + subordinate clause: It is believed that...
2. Subject + is + V3 + to-infinitive: She is believed to be...

Used in news and official texts.
mistakes: It is said that prices will risen → will rise
He is believed to be honest → He is believed to be honest
ex: It is reported that three people were injured.
Three people were reported injured.
He is thought to have left the country.
He is believed to have left the country.

b2_12.text: remember / forget + V-ing vs to-inf - difference in meaning
b2_12.note: I remember locking it (fact of the past) vs Remember to lock it (task)
b2_12.exp:
remember/forget + V-ing - about a past event that we remember/forgot.
remember/forget + to-inf - about the task: remember to do something in the future.
mistakes: I remember to lock it (if the fact is past) → I remember locking it
Remember locking it! (task) → Remember to lock it!
I forgot doing the homework (if you haven’t done it) → I forgot to do the homework
ex: I remember meeting her at a conference. (это было)
I remember meeting her at a conference.
Remember to call your mother! (нужно сделать)
Don't forget to call your mom!
I forgot to buy milk. (не сделал)
I forgot to buy milk.

b2_13.text: stop / regret / mean + V-ing vs to-inf
b2_13.note: She stopped smoking vs She stopped to smoke
b2_13.exp:
stop + V-ing - stop the action.
stop + to-inf - stop to do something else.

regret + V-ing - to regret the past.
regret + to-inf - regret to inform you (formally): I regret to inform you...

mean + V-ing - mean: This means working harder.
mean + to-inf - intend: I meant to call you.
mistakes: She stopped to smoke (if quit) → She stopped smoking
She stopped smoking to answer (stopped to) → She stopped to answer
ex: He stopped smoking last year.
He quit smoking last year.
She stopped to look at the view.
She stopped to admire the view.

b2_14.text: wish + Past Simple - desire to change the present
b2_14.note: I wish I knew the answer. I wish I had more time.
b2_14.exp:
wish + Past Simple - about an unrealistic desire in the present.
The form is the same as in the 2nd type of conditional. For to be - were (formally), although was is also used.
mistakes: I wish I know the answer → I wish I knew
I wish I was taller - perhaps, but formally: I wish I were taller
I wish I would know - with would only about others: I wish you would listen
ex: I wish I spoke better English.
It's a pity that I don't speak English better.
She wishes she lived in a warmer country.
She would like to live in a warmer country.

b2_15.text: wish + Past Perfect - regret about the past
b2_15.note: I wish I had studied harder. I wish I hadn't said that.
b2_15.exp: wish + Past Perfect - regret about past events that cannot be changed. The form is as in the 3rd type of conditional.
mistakes: I wish I didn't say that → I wish I hadn't said (regret about the past = Past Perfect)
I wish she had took that job → she had taken
ex: I wish I hadn't eaten so much.
It's a shame I ate so much.
She wishes she had taken that job offer.
She wishes she had accepted that job offer.

b2_16.text: wish + would - desire to change someone else's behavior
b2_16.note: I wish you would stop talking. I wish it would warm up.
b2_16.exp:
wish + would - irritation due to someone else's behavior or a desire to change the situation.
⚠️ You cannot use about yourself: I wish I would - only about other persons or the weather/situation.
mistakes: I wish I would stop (about myself) → you can’t! Use: I wish I could stop
I wish you stop talking → I wish you would stop
ex: I wish you would listen to me.
I wish you would listen to me.
I wish it would stop raining.
I wish the rain would stop.

b2_17.note: The man who called (determines which one) vs My brother, who called, (adds info)
b2_17.exp:
Defining (defining): without commas - they clarify who/what they are talking about. The pronoun can be replaced with that. It cannot be removed without losing the meaning.

Non-defining: with commas - add information without changing the meaning. That cannot be used. Can be removed.

⚠️ which in non-determiners can refer to the entire sentence: She passed the exam, which surprised everyone.
mistakes: My brother, that called, is a doctor → My brother, who called (you can’t use that in a non-defining one)
<s>The man who lives next door, is very kind</s> → without comma (defining clause)
ex: The film that I told you about is on tonight. (какой именно)
The movie I told you about is showing tonight.
My sister, who lives in Paris, is visiting next week. (доп. инфо)
My sister, who lives in Paris, will arrive next week.

b2_18.note: the person I work with / the person with whom I work (formally)
b2_18.exp:
In colloquial speech, the preposition comes at the end: the house I grew up in.
In written/formal speech, the preposition before which/whom: the house in which I grew up.

After the preposition - only which (for things) and whom (for people). Never that!
mistakes: The house which I grew up in it → extra it
The person which I spoke → the person I spoke to
ex: The project I'm working on is fascinating. (разг.)
The project I'm working on is very interesting.
The project on which I am working is fascinating. (форм.)

b2_19.text: Consequence conjunctions: so... that, such... that, therefore, as a result
b2_19.note: It was so cold that we stayed inside. She worked hard; therefore, she passed.
b2_19.exp:
so + adjective/adverb + that: He spoke so quickly that nobody understood.
such + (a/an) + adjective + noun + that: It was such a long film that I fell asleep.

Connecting words of result (between sentences): therefore, consequently, as a result, hence, thus.
mistakes: It was so cold that we stayed - right! But: It was such cold that → It was such a cold day
<s>Such was great his relief</s> → So great was his relief (with adjective - so)
ex: It was such a good book that I read it twice.
The book was so good that I read it twice.
She missed the deadline; consequently, she lost the contract.
She missed the deadline; As a result, she lost her contract.

b2_20.text: be used to / get used to + V-ing - habit (real)
b2_20.note: I'm used to waking up early. She's getting used to the cold.
b2_20.exp:
be used to + V-ing / noun = to be used to (already used to).
get used to + V-ing / noun = get used to (the process of getting used to).

⚠️ Not to be confused with used to + infinitive (past habits).
• I used to live in Paris. - used to live (no longer)
• I am used to living in big cities. - used to living in big cities
mistakes: I'm used to wake up early → I'm used to waking up (be used to + V-ing)
I get used to woke up → I get used to waking up
ex: I'm not used to getting up so early.
I'm not used to getting up so early.
It took time, but she got used to the new system.
It took time, but she got used to the new system.

b2_21.text: Future in the Past: would / was going to
b2_21.note: She said she would call. He was going to leave.
b2_21.exp:
Future in the Past - constructions that express the future from the point of view of the past. Used in indirect speech and narrative.

• would + V (from will): She said she would come.
• was/were going to + V: He was going to call but forgot.
• was about to + V: She was about to leave when he arrived.
mistakes: She said she will come → She said she would come (time shift)
He was going to called → He was going to call
ex: She promised she would be there.
She promised that she would come.
He was about to leave when she called.
He was about to leave when she called.

b2_22.text: be to - official appointment and order
b2_22.note: You are to report by Monday. They were never to meet again.
b2_22.exp:
be to + infinitive - official orders, planned events and fate in the narrative.

1. Legal Instruction: Passengers are to remain seated.
2. Official plan: The summit is to take place next month.
3. Fate in the narrative: They were never to meet again.
mistakes: You be to submit → You are to submit
She was never to meet → She was never to meet
ex: You are to submit the report by Friday.
You must submit your report by Friday.
She was to become one of the greatest scientists of her time.
She was destined to become one of the greatest scientists.

b2_23.text: ought to - moral duty and logical expectation
b2_23.note: You ought to apologise. She ought to have told us.
b2_23.exp:
ought to is a moral obligation or logical expectation. Stronger should.
Always with to: ought to go (as opposed to should).
For the past: ought to have + V3 - reproach or regret.
mistakes: You ought apologise → You ought to apologise (ought always with to!)
She ought to have told us → She ought to have told us
ex: You ought to apologise for what you said.
You should apologize for what you said.
She ought to have told us earlier.
She should have told us sooner.

b2_24.note: You needn't worry. / She doesn't need to come.
b2_24.exp:
need as a modal (formal, in questions/negations) and as a regular verb.

Modal (without -s, without to):
• You needn't worry. / Need I explain?

Regular need + to:
• She doesn't need to come.
mistakes: <s>You needn't to worry</s> → You needn't worry (modal needn't without to)
She need come → She needs to come (regular need + to)
ex: You needn't fill in both forms.
You do not need to fill out both forms.
She doesn't need to attend every meeting.
She does not have to attend every meeting.

b2_25.note: How dare you! I daren't ask. She dared to challenge him.
b2_25.exp:
dare - dare, dare. Modal in rhetorical questions and negations.

Modal: How dare you! / I daren't ask. / Dare I say...
Normal: She didn't dare to look. / He dared to challenge the boss.

⚠️ I dare say = perhaps, probably (stable expression).
mistakes: <s>How dare you to say</s> → How dare you say (after dare without to)
I dare say it is normal; I dare say = perhaps
ex: How dare you speak to me like that!
How dare you talk to me like that!
She dared to express her opinion openly.
She dared to openly express her opinion.

b2_26.note: I hurt myself. She did it herself. They enjoyed themselves.
b2_26.exp:
myself, yourself, yourself, yourself, yourself, yourself, yourselves, yourself.

1. The action is directed towards the subject: He cut himself.
2. Emphase (by yourself, without help): I did it myself.

Stable: by myself = alone; help yourself = help yourself.
mistakes: She taught her to play (if herself) → She taught herself to play
They enjoyed themself → They enjoyed themselves
I did it by my own → I did it by myself
ex: She taught herself to play the guitar.
She taught herself to play the guitar.
The machine turns itself off automatically.
The machine turns off automatically.

b2_27.note: The team are playing well. (BrE) / The team is playing. (AmE)
b2_27.exp:
Collective nouns are a group as a whole.

• BrE: usually plural. number: The team are playing.
• AmE: usually units. number: The team is playing.

Frequent: team, family, government, committee, staff, audience, crew, police, army, public, management
mistakes: The police is here → The police are here (plural in BrE)
The team is playing well - true in AmE; are true in BrE
ex: The government have announced new measures. (BrE)
The government announced new measures.
The audience were on their feet. (BrE)
The audience jumped to their feet.

b2_28.note: a half, three quarters, 5.7 = five point seven, 25%
b2_28.exp:
Fractions:½ = a half; ⅓ = a third; ¼ = a quarter; ¾ = three quarters; ⅔ = two thirds.
Numerator is quantitative, denominator is ordinal (plural if numerator > 1).

Decimals: period (not comma): 3.14 = three point one four.
Mathematics: + = plus; - = minus; × = times; ÷ = divided by; = = equals.
mistakes: Two third of students → Two thirds of students
3,14 → 3.14 (period, not comma)
25 percents → 25 percent (without s)
ex: Three quarters of students passed the exam.
Three quarters of the students passed the exam.
The inflation rate fell to 2.5 percent.
The inflation rate dropped to 2.5 percent.

b2_29.note: faster, more carefully, best, worse, further
b2_29.exp:
Adverbs form degrees of comparison according to the same rules as adjectives.

• Monosyllabic: + er/est: fast→faster, hard→harder, early→earlier
• Most on -ly: more/most: carefully→more carefully→most carefully
• Incorrect: well→better→best, badly→worse→worst, far→further→furthest, little→less→least, much→more→most
mistakes: She sings more beautiful → She sings more beautifully
He runs more fast → He runs faster
She works most hardly → She works hardest
ex: She spoke more confidently than before.
She spoke more confidently than before.
He works hardest in the team.
He works harder than anyone on the team.

c1_01.note: Never have I seen this. Rarely does she complain.
c1_01.exp:
For emphasis, the negative adverb/expression is placed at the beginning → auxiliary verb before the subject (word order as in a question).

Words that trigger inversion: never, rarely, rarely, little, hardly, scarcely, barely, not only, only, no sooner
mistakes: Never I have seen → Never have I seen (inversion: auxiliary before subject)
Rarely she complains → Rarely does she complain
ex: Never have I seen anything so beautiful.
I have never seen anything so beautiful.
Rarely does she make a mistake.
She rarely makes mistakes.
Little did I know what was coming.
I had no idea what was waiting for me.

c1_02.text: Not only... but also with inversion
c1_02.note: Not only did he apologise, but he also offered to help.
c1_02.exp:
Not only + inversion in the first part, the second part is the usual order.
Used for emphasis: expresses that something went further than expected.
mistakes: Not only he apologised → Not only did he apologise
Not only she is talented but also is hardworking → but she is also hardworking (the second part is the usual order)
ex: Not only is she talented, but she is also very hard-working.
She is not only talented, but also very hardworking.
Not only did they arrive late, but they also forgot the documents.
They were not only late, but also forgot their documents.

c1_03.note: Hardly had I sat down when the phone rang.
c1_03.exp:
Immediate sequence constructions:
• Hardly/Scarcely + had + subject + V3 + when/before + Past Simple
• No sooner + had + subject + V3 + than + Past Simple
mistakes: Hardly I had sat → Hardly had I sat
No sooner I sat than → No sooner had I sat than
ex: Hardly had she arrived when it started to rain.
She had barely arrived when it started to rain.
No sooner had I sat down than someone knocked at the door.
I had just sat down when someone knocked on the door.

c1_04.text: Inversion in conditionals: Had / Were / Should
c1_04.note: Had I known (= If I had known). Were I you (= If I were you).
c1_04.exp:
Formal style - excludes if, uses inversion:
• Had + subject + V3 = If + Past Perfect (3rd type)
• Were + subject (+ to + infinitive) = If + Past Simple (2nd type)
• Should + subject + infinitive = If (unlikely, type 1)
mistakes: Have I known, I would have helped → Had I known
Should you need help, please to call → please call (without to after please)
ex: Had she told me, I would have helped.
If she had told me, I would have helped.
Were I in your position, I would accept.
If I were you, I would agree.
Should you need assistance, please call us.
If you need assistance, call us.

c1_05.text: It-cleft: It was John who called.
c1_05.note: To highlight any member of a sentence
c1_05.exp:
Structure: It + to be + selected element + who/that/which + rest
Shifts the focus of attention to the highlighted element.

• For people - who; for things/circumstances - that/which.
mistakes: <s>It was John who he called</s> → It was John who called (extra he)
It was the noise which woke me - correct, but that is preferable for things
ex: It was the noise that woke me up. (не шум вообще, именно этот)
It was the noise that woke me up.
It is hard work that leads to success.
It is hard work that leads to success.

c1_06.text: Wh-cleft: What surprised me was the price.
c1_06.note: What I need is rest. What she said shocked everyone.
c1_06.exp:
Structure: What + subordinate clause + to be + highlighted element
Emphasizes the importance of the element introduced at the end.
mistakes: What I love it is London → What I love is London (extra it)
What surprised me were the price → What surprised me was the price (singular number)
ex: What I love about London is the diversity.
What I like most about London is the diversity.
What he did was completely unexpected.
What he did was completely unexpected.

c1_07.text: Formal subjunctive after verbs of demand
c1_07.note: I suggest he leave. It is vital that she be informed.
c1_07.exp:
After suggest, recommend, insist, demand, propose, request, require, ask, advise, order + that - the basic form of the verb for all persons (without -s, without was).

The British version often uses should instead of the subjunctive: I suggest that he should leave.
American - often a pure subjunctive.
mistakes: I suggest that he leaves → I suggest that he leave (subjunctive - without -s!)
It is essential that she is informed → that she be informed
ex: I recommend that he see a doctor.
I recommend that he see a doctor.
It is essential that all students attend the meeting.
It is essential that all students attend the meeting.

c1_08.note: It's high time you went to bed. It's time we left.
c1_08.exp:
It's (high/about) time + subject + Past Simple
Meaning: present/future - we are talking about something that should have been done a long time ago.
high time - even stronger: it’s high time!
mistakes: It's high time you will go → It's high time you went (Past Simple!)
It's high time for you go → It's high time you went
ex: It's time she found a new job.
It's high time she found a new job.
It's high time you apologised.
It's high time to apologize.

c1_09.note: She talks as if she knew everything. He looked as though he had seen a ghost.
c1_09.exp:
as if / as though + Past Simple is an unrealistic comparison in the present.
as if / as though + Past Perfect is an unrealistic comparison about the past.
mistakes: She talks as if she knows everything (unreal situation) → as if she knew
He looked as if he saw a ghost → as if he had seen
ex: He spends money as if he were a millionaire.
He spends money like he's a millionaire.
She spoke as though she had met him before.
She spoke as if she had met him before.

c1_10.note: So do I. Neither does she. So am I.
c1_10.exp:
So + auxiliary + subject - agreement with the affirmative.
Neither/Nor + auxiliary + subject - agreement with the negative.
The auxiliary verb must coincide with the tense of the original sentence.
mistakes: So I do → So do I (auxiliary before subject)
Neither she does → Neither does she
I love jazz. So she does too → So does she
ex: I love jazz. So does she.
I love jazz. She too.
I haven't been to Rome. Neither have I.
I haven't been to Rome. Me too.

c1_11.text: I think so / I hope so / I'm afraid so
c1_11.note: Will it rain? - I think so. / I hope not.
c1_11.exp:
