so replaces the subordinate clause after: think, hope, suppose, expect, believe, imagine, be afraid.
Negative form: not (not so not!).
mistakes: <s>I think so not</s> → I don't think so
I hope so not → I hope not
ex: Will he come? — I think so. / I don't think so.
Will he come? - I think yes. / Don't think.
Is it expensive? — I'm afraid so.
Is it expensive? - I'm afraid so.
Is it closed? — I hope not.
Closed? - I hope not.

c1_12.note: The man who called is my brother. VS My brother, who called, is a doctor.
c1_12.exp:
Defining: specifies who/what we are talking about - without commas. that is possible. The pronoun can be omitted if it is an object.

Non-defining: adds information - commas required. Only who/which (not that!). The pronoun cannot be omitted.
mistakes: <s>The film that won the Oscar, was brilliant</s> → without comma (defining clause)
Avatar, that came out in 2009 → Avatar, which came out (you can’t use that in a non-defining one!)
ex: The film that won the Oscar was brilliant. (определяет, о каком фильме речь)
The film that won the Oscar was great.
Avatar, which came out in 2009, was a huge hit. (доп. факт о и так известном фильме)
Avatar, released in 2009, became a big hit.

c1_13.note: The company for which I work. The person with whom I spoke.
c1_13.exp:
Formal style: preposition + which/whom before a relative pronoun.
Informal equivalent: preposition at the end of a sentence.
whom is the formal object form of who.
mistakes: <s>The company which I work for it</s> → extra it
The person who I spoke → the person I spoke to / to whom I spoke
ex: The report to which I referred is attached. (формально)
The report I mentioned is attached.
The report which I referred to is attached. (нейтрально)
The report I referred to is attached. (разговорно)

c1_14.exp:
Nominalization is the conversion of verbs/adjectives into nouns. A sign of academic and business style.

Main suffixes:
• -tion/-sion: decide→decision, discuss→discussion
• -ment: improve→improvement, develop→development
• -ance/-ence: appear→appearance, differ→difference
• -ity: complex→complexity, able→ability
• -ness: happy→happiness, aware→awareness
mistakes: I made a decision to expand → correct, but the style is weaker: Our decision to expand (nominalization)
He discovered an error → His discovery of the error
ex: She decided to expand. → Our decision to expand the company...
Our decision to expand...
He discovered that... → His discovery of the error...
His discovery of the error...

c1_15.note: The fact that prices increased → The increase in prices...
c1_15.exp:
Nominalization allows:
1. Compress information: There was a significant increase in prices.
2. Add definitions: The rapid increase in house prices...
3. Create a formal, impersonal tone.
mistakes: There was a significant increase → a significant increase (adjective before a noun)
The prices' increase → The increase in prices
ex: There has been a significant improvement in air quality.
Air quality has improved significantly.
His refusal to comment surprised everyone.
His refusal to comment surprised everyone.

c1_16.note: Walking down the street, I saw an old friend. Having no money, he couldn't buy food.
c1_16.exp:
The participial phrase with Present Participle (V-ing) replaces the subordinate clause:
• Simultaneity: Walking home, I noticed something strange. (= While I was walking home)
• Reason: Knowing the answer, she raised her hand. (= Because she knew)

⚠️ The subject of the participial phrase MUST coincide with the subject of the main sentence!
Error: Walking down the street, the rain started. (the rain did not fall on the street)
mistakes: Walking down the street, the rain started → subject ≠ subject. Correct: Walking down the street, I got wet.
Knowing the answer, her hand was raised → the subject must match: Knowing the answer, she raised her hand.
ex: Arriving at the airport, he realised he had forgotten his passport.
Arriving at the airport, he realized that he had forgotten his passport.
Not knowing what to do, she called her mother.
Not knowing what to do, she called her mother.

c1_17.text: Past participial phrase: V3 / Having + V3 clause
c1_17.note: Written in 1815, the novel... Having finished work, she went home.
c1_17.exp:
V3 clause (Past Participle) = passive meaning: Built in 1889, the Eiffel Tower...

Having + V3 = action completed before the main one:
Having read the report, he called a meeting. (= After he had read...)
mistakes: Having read the report, the meeting was called → who read it? It should be: Having read the report, he called a meeting.
Surprised by the news, she was sat down → she herself: she sat down
ex: Surprised by the news, she sat down silently.
Surprised by the news, she sat down silently.
Having finished the exam, the students left the room.
Having finished the exam, the students left the classroom.

c2_01.text: Conditional with unless / provided / as long as / on condition that
c2_01.note: Unless you study, you'll fail. As long as you're honest, I'll help.
c2_01.exp:
unless = if not (but cannot be used with negation!): Unless you hurry = If you don't hurry
provided/providing (that) = if and only if (strict condition)
as long as = provided that (assumption)
on condition that = on condition that (formally)
in case = in case
mistakes: <s>Unless you don't study</s> → Unless you study (unless = if not, double negative is unnecessary)
As long as you'll be honest → As long as you are honest
ex: I'll lend you money, as long as you pay me back.
I'll lend you money on the condition that you pay it back.
You can use my laptop, provided you don't download anything.
You can use my laptop, as long as you don't download anything.

c2_02.note: Suppose you won the lottery - what would you do?
c2_02.exp:
Suppose/Supposing - used as if in hypothetical questions.
What if is an informal analogue.

With Past Simple - a situation is unlikely or imaginary.
mistakes: Suppose you would win → Suppose you won (Past Simple for hypothesis)
ex: Supposing you had to choose — which would you pick?
Imagine that you had to choose - what would you choose?
What if nobody comes? What should we do?
What if no one comes? What should we do?

c2_03.note: Only when I left did I realize. Only then did he understand.
c2_03.exp:
Only + when/after/if/then/by/in/on - the entire group is moved to the beginning → inversion.

This is one of the most powerful rhetorical devices in writing and public speaking.
mistakes: Only when I left, I realized → Only when I left did I realise (inversion!)
Only after years of practice, she mastered → ... did she master
ex: Only when you experience it can you truly understand.
Only by experiencing it can one truly understand.
Only after years of practice did she master the skill.
It was only after years of practice that she mastered the skill.

c2_04.note: So great was his relief that he wept. Such was her talent...
c2_04.exp:
Book and oratorical style.
So + adj/adv + be/aux + subject
Such + was/were + subject
mistakes: So great his relief was that → So great was his relief
Such great was her talent → Such was her talent / So great was her talent
ex: So rapid was the change that nobody could adapt.
The changes happened so quickly that no one could adapt.
Such was her talent that she was offered a scholarship.
So great was her talent that she was offered a scholarship.

c2_05.text: Hedging: appear to, seem to, tend to, be likely to
c2_05.note: Prices appear to be rising. This tends to occur when...
c2_05.exp:
Hedging is the deliberate softening of statements to express scientific modesty.

Main structures:
• appear/seem to - apparently
• tend to - as a rule, tend to
• be likely/unlikely to - likely/unlikely
• be thought/considered to be - it is believed that
mistakes: Prices appear rising → Prices appear to be rising
Results seem suggest → Results seem to suggest
ex: The results appear to suggest a correlation.
The results appear to indicate a correlation.
Companies tend to underestimate implementation costs.
Companies tend to underestimate implementation costs.

c2_06.note: Furthermore, Nevertheless, Conversely, In light of, With regard to
c2_06.exp:
Markers structure and connect academic text:

• Addition: Moreover, Furthermore, In addition, Additionally
• Contrast: However, nevertheless, nevertheless, Conversely, On the other hand
• Result: Therefore, Consequently, As a result, Hence, Thus
• Explanation: In other words, That is to say, Namely
• Concession: Admittedly, While it is true that, Despite this
mistakes: furthermore/moreover confusion - both add, but moreover is stronger
<s>Conversely, this shows the same</s> - conversely introduces contrast, not confirmation
ex: The experiment failed. Nevertheless, the findings were instructive.
The experiment failed. Still, the results were instructive.
Furthermore, the data suggests a strong correlation.
Moreover, the data suggests a strong correlation.

c2_07.note: I would like to enquire... vs I'd like to ask... vs Can I ask...
c2_07.exp:
Register is determined by purpose, audience and context.

Formal: passive, nominalization, complex conjunctions, full forms, verbs of Latin origin (commence, terminate, assist).
Neutral: standard grammar, no slang, moderate abbreviations.
Informal: ellipsis, phrasal verbs (put off = postpone), contractions, colloquial expressions.
mistakes: Mixing registers: formal beginning and colloquial ending in one text
I would like to ask about this thing → this matter (formal case)
ex: Formal: I wish to draw your attention to a discrepancy.
Neutral: I want to point out a mistake.
Informal: Just wanted to flag something up.

c2_08.text: Connotations of synonyms - shades of meaning
c2_08.exp:
Synonyms differ in connotation (emotional coloring) and register.

Examples of scales:
• slim (+) → thin (0) → skinny (-) → scrawny/gaunt (--)
• determined (+) → firm (0) → stubborn/pig-headed (-)
• thrifty (+) → economical (0) → stingy/tight-fisted (-)
• confident (+) → assertive (0) → arrogant (-)
mistakes: She is very thin (neutral) vs intentional slim (+) or skinny (−)
Using a word with the wrong connotation when giving a compliment: stubborn instead of determined
ex: She is slim. (+) / She is thin. (0) / She is skinny. (-)
Same thing, but different attitude.

c2_09.note: "We shall fight on the beaches, we shall fight on the landing grounds..." - anaphora
c2_09.exp:
Anaphora: repetition of words at the beginning of successive sentences. "I have a dream... I have a dream..."
Chiasmus: AB-BA cross structure. "Ask not what your country can do for you, but what you can do for your country."
Tricolon: three parallel elements. "Veni, vidi, vici." / "Government of the people, by the people, for the people."

These techniques are used in oratory, essays and journalism.
mistakes: Using anaphora without parallel structure disrupts the rhythm
Tricolon with unequal elements: "fast, efficiently, with great care" → "fast, efficient, and careful"
ex: Friends, Romans, countrymen, lend me your ears. (триколон)
The more you learn, the more you earn. (хиазм в народной мудрости)

c2_10.note: conduct research, draw conclusions, reach a consensus, address an issue
c2_10.exp:
In C2 it is important to use the 'correct' verb with each noun.

Academic collocations:
• conduct/carry out research (not make/do)
• draw/reach a conclusion
• raise/address/tackle an issue
• reach/achieve a consensus
• make significant progress
• pose/present a challenge
mistakes: make research → conduct/carry out research
do a conclusion → draw/reach a conclusion
make an issue → raise/address an issue
ex: Researchers conducted extensive interviews.
The researchers conducted extensive interviews.
The committee failed to reach a consensus.
The committee was unable to reach consensus.

c2_11.note: Weather permitting, we'll have a picnic. Her eyes filled with tears, she left the room.
c2_11.exp:
Absolute construction = noun/pronoun + participle (independent subject).
Used in written and formal style - adds a circumstance without a conjunction.

Types:
• Weather permitting = If the weather permits
• All things considered = If you consider everything
• Her work finished = When/After her work was finished
• This done = When/After this was done
mistakes: <s>Weather permitting, the picnic will be had by us</s> → redundant passive
Her eyes filling with tears, she left is acceptable, but Her eyes filled is more accurate for a completed fact
ex: All things considered, it was a successful event.
All things considered, it was a successful event.
The deadline having passed, the project was cancelled.
After the deadline passed, the project was cancelled.

c2_12.text: Ellipsis and substitution in connected text
c2_12.note: I wanted to go but didn't (want to). She said she'd call and she did.
c2_12.exp:
Ellipsis is the omission of already known elements.
Substitution - using do/so/one/it instead of repeating.

Ellipse types:
• A: Are you coming? B: Might (do).
• She speaks French and he does too / so does he.
• I wanted to leave, but wasn't allowed to. (leave omitted)

Substitution: I thought he'd pass. He did. / The big one? I prefer the small one
mistakes: I wanted to go but didn't want to → but didn't (we omit want to)
She said she'd call and she said → and she did (substitution through do)
ex: Can you drive? — I used to (drive).
Can you drive? - I used to be able to.
She said she'd be here and she was.
She said she would come, and she came.

c2_13.text: Infinitive phrases instead of subordinate clauses
c2_13.note: I want you to explain. She asked me to help. He doesn't seem to be happy.
c2_13.exp:
Complex infinitive (Complex Object) replaces the subordinate clause:
I want + object + to-infinitive: I want her to stay.

After see, hear, watch, let, make, have - infinitive without to:
I saw her leave. / She made him cry. / Let me help.
In the passive - with to: He was made to pay.

Constructions with seem, appear, happen, prove + to-inf:
She seems to know. / He happened to be there.
mistakes: I saw her to leave → I saw her leave (after see without to)
<s>She was made to leave</s> - that's right! In the passive - with to
I want that she stays → I want her to stay
ex: I need you to sign this document.
I need you to sign this document.
She was made to apologise publicly.
She was forced to publicly apologize.
He seemed to have forgotten everything.
He seemed to have forgotten everything.

c2_14.text: Future Perfect Continuous: will have been + V-ing
c2_14.note: By next year, I will have been studying English for five years.
c2_14.exp:
Future Perfect Continuous = will have been + V-ing

Emphasis on the duration of an action that will last until a certain point in the future. Often answers the question “How long by then?”

Often used with by (the time), for, when: By Monday, she will have been working on this project for three weeks.
mistakes: By next year I will be studying → I will have been studying
She will have been work → She will have been working
ex: By next year, I will have been learning English for five years.
By next year I will have been studying English for five years.
When we arrive, she will have been waiting for two hours.
When we arrive, she will already have been waiting for two hours.

c2_15.text: Coordination of tenses - sequence of tenses
c2_15.note: She said she had been working. He thought it would be difficult.
c2_15.exp:
In complex sentences, the verb of the subordinate clause agrees in time with the main one.

If the main verb is in the past:
• Present Simple → Past Simple: he said it was true
• Past Simple → Past Perfect: she said she had seen it
• Present Perfect → Past Perfect: he said he had finished
• will → would; can → could; may → might; is → was

⚠️ Exception: general truths and scientific facts do not change: She said that water boils at 100°C.
mistakes: She said that water is boiling (fact) → water boils
He said he saw it → He said he had seen it
ex: He told me that he had been living there for years.
He told me that he had lived there for many years.
She said the Earth moves around the Sun. (общая истина — не меняем)
She said that the Earth moves around the Sun.

c2_16.text: Coordinating conjunctions: and, but, or, nor, for, yet, so
c2_16.note: FANBOYS: For, And, Nor, But, Or, Yet, So
c2_16.exp:
Coordinating conjunctions connect equivalent parts of a sentence.

Seven Basic (acronym FANBOYS):
• for = because (formal): She left, for she was tired.
• and = and (adding)
• nor = and not (Neither/Nor... inversion!): She didn't call, nor did she write.
• but = but (contrast)
• or = or (alternative)
• yet = however (contrast, more formal but)
• so = therefore (consequence)
mistakes: <s>She was tired, yet kept working</s> - correct without “she” in the second part
She didn't call nor she wrote → nor did she write (inversion!)
ex: She was tired, yet she kept working.
She was tired, but continued to work.
He didn't study, nor did he attend class.
He didn't study or go to classes.

c2_17.text: Subordinating conjunctions: although, whereas, provided, unless...
c2_17.note: although, as though, whereas, provided that, unless, given that, in case
c2_17.exp:
Subordinating conjunctions introduce subordinate clauses.

By value:
• Time: when, while, as, after, before, until, once, as soon as, whenever
• Reasons: because, since, as, given that, seeing that
• Conditions: if, unless, provided, as long as, in case, supposing
• Goals: so that, in order that, best
• Concessions: although, even though, whereas, while, however
mistakes: Although the rain, we went → Although it was raining
<s>Seeing that the deadline passed</s> → had passed (Past Perfect before another past)
ex: Seeing that the deadline had passed, we cancelled the meeting.
Since the deadline passed, we canceled the meeting.
Lest she forget, he sent her a reminder.
So that she wouldn't forget, he sent her a reminder.

c2_18.note: Use a comma before FANBOYS. Use a semicolon to link related clauses.
c2_18.exp:
Comma:
• Before coordinating conjunctions FANBOYS between independent clauses.
• After introductory words/turns of phrase: However, she decided to stay.
• In the enumerations (Oxford comma - before the last and): apples, oranges, and bananas.

Semicolon (;): connects two independent clauses without a conjunction.
She was tired; she went to bed

Colon (:): introduces a list, explanation, quotation.
mistakes: However she decided to stay → However, she decided
She was tired; and she went to bed → without a conjunction after the semicolon: She was tired; she went to bed
ex: However, the results were inconclusive; further research is required.
However, the results are mixed; further research is required.
The company has three priorities: efficiency, innovation, and sustainability.
The company has three priorities: efficiency, innovation and sustainability.

c2_19.note: Em dash - for emphasis - sets off a phrase. It's vs its. "Direct speech"
c2_19.exp:
Dash (—): highlights a plug-in construction (stronger than a comma): The solution - though expensive - proven effective.

Apostrophe:
• Abbreviations: it's = it is; don't; they're
• Possessive case: John's book; the students' results
• ⚠️ its (belonging) vs it's (it is)

Quotes: AmE - double ("text"), BrE - single ('text').
mistakes: Its a big house → It's a big house
The cats tail → The cat's tail
Her's book → Her book (possessive pronouns do not have an apostrophe)
ex: The project — launched in 2020 — exceeded all expectations.
The project, launched in 2020, exceeded all expectations.
It's important to check its settings before use.
It is important to check its settings before using it.

c2_20.text: Special and indirect questions in academic text
c2_20.note: The question is whether... / I wonder what the data shows.
c2_20.exp:
In academic texts, direct questions are replaced by indirect ones (interrogative clauses).

An indirect question is a subordinate clause with the usual word order (without inversion, without do).

Introductory words: whether, if (yes/no), what, where, when, how, why, which.

Example: What does this data show? → The question is what this data shows.
mistakes: I wonder what does the data show → what the data shows (no inversion in an indirect question)
The question is whether does he know → whether he knows
ex: I wonder whether the hypothesis is correct.
I wonder if the hypothesis is true.
The study examines how social media affects behaviour.
The study examines how social media influences behavior.
